kh:body.kh:document-title: The key forms of ISDA credit support documents
kh:body.kh:mini-summary: This Practice Note describes what the key forms of ISDA credit support documents are and the key differences between the English law credit support annex, English law credit support deed and New York law credit support annex.

tr:secmain.core:title: What does this Practice Note cover?
tr:secmain.core:para: This Practice Note describes:

core:listitem.core:para: what the key forms of ISDA credit support documents are, and
the key differences between the English law credit support annex, English law credit support deed and New York law credit support annex

tr:secmain.core:title: What are the key forms of ISDA credit support document?
tr:secmain.core:para: Credit support is a means of providing collateral or a security interest for payment obligations under derivative transactions (see Practice Note: What is credit support and how is it used in the ISDA credit support documentation). The International Swaps and Derivatives Association, Inc (ISDA) has produced standard form credit support documentation which either takes the form of:

core:listitem.core:para: a credit support annex—known as a CSA, or
a credit support deed—known as a CSD

tr:secmain.core:para: The key forms of credit support documents published by ISDA are:

core:listitem.core:para: 1995 ISDA Credit Support Annex (Transfer—English law)—known as the English law CSA or the English law annex (the English law Anne—for more information, see Practice Note: ISDA Credit Support Annex: Transfer—English law)
2013 ISDA Standard Credit Support Annex (Transfer—English law) (the English law SCSA)

ISDA 2016 Credit Support Annex for Variation Margin (VM) (Title Transfer—English Law) (the 2016 CSA VM)—for more information, see Practice Note: ISDA 2016 Credit Support Annex for Variation Margin (VM)—clause by clause guide

1995 ISDA Credit Support Deed (Security Interest—English law)—known as the English law CSD or the English law deed (the English law Deed—for more information, see Practice Note: ISDA Credit Support Deed: Security Interest—English law)
ISDA 2016 Phase One Initial Margin (IM) Credit Support Deed (Security Interest—English Law)

1994 ISDA Credit Support Annex (Security Interest—New York law)—known as the New York law CSA or the New York law annex (the New York law Annex—for more information, see Practice Note: ISDA Credit Support Annex: Security Interest—New York law)
ISDA 2016 Phase One Credit Support Annex for IM (Security Interest—New York Law)
ISDA 2016 Credit Support Annex for Variation Margin (VM) (Security Interest—New York Law)
2013 ISDA Credit Support Annex (Security Interest—New York law) (the New York law SCSA)
2018 ISDA Credit Support Annex for Initial Margin (IM) (Security Interest—New York Law)
2018 Credit Support Deed for Initial Margin (IM) (Security Interest—English Law)
2019 Bank Custodian Collateral Transfer Agreement for Initial Margin and forms of Security Agreement

tr:secmain.core:para: There are also additional credit support documents published by ISDA including the 1995 Credit Support Annex (Japanese law), ISDA 2016 Credit Support Annex for Variation Margin (VM) (Loan—Japanese Law) and ISDA/IIFM 2017 Credit Support Deed for Cash Collateral (VM) but these are not considered in this Practice Note.

ISDA also published the 1995 ISDA Credit Support Annex (Title Transfer—Irish Law) and 1995 ISDA Credit Support Annex (Title Transfer—French Law) in connection with the new Irish and French law governed 2002 ISDA Master Agreements respectively, which were published in 2018 as part of an ISDA initiative in response to member demand for documentation governed by the laws of an EU Member State in light of Brexit.

tr:secmain.core:title: Which is the correct ISDA credit support document to use?
tr:secmain.core:para: The English law Annex, English law SCSA and the English law Deed are intended for use with an ISDA Master Agreement that selects English law as its governing law. The New York law Annex and New York law SCSA are intended for use with an ISDA Master Agreement that selects New York law as its governing law. It is usual, although not mandatory, to select a credit support document with a governing law that matches the governing law of the relevant master agreement.

It is possible to use a New York law Annex with an English law governed master agreement because although the New York law Annex is an annex to the schedule to an ISDA Master Agreement that selects New York law, it functions as an independent security document. Some adjustments to the wording of the New York law Annex are however required and specific legal advice should be taken on this point.

It is highly inadvisable to combine an English law Annex with a New York law governed ISDA master agreement without taking specific legal advice and significantly redrafting the English law Annex. The English law Annex functions as an annex to the schedule to an ISDA master agreement that selects English law as its governing law. It feeds in to the close-out netting provisions in the ISDA master agreement. The consequences of tampering with this mechanism, without careful consideration or legal advice, could be severe.

The English law SCSA and New York law SCSA are standardised versions of the English law Annex and New York law Annex. They remove the optionality found in the English law Annex and New York law Annex, encourage the adoption of overnight index swap discounting and create a homogenous valuation framework. However, they otherwise reflect the general features and principles found in the English law Annex and New York law Annex.
The credit support documents are all available on the ISDA Books website or the ISDA MyLibrary platform, for a fee.

tr:secmain.core:title: ISDA credit support documents—a quick compare and contrast

tr:secsub1.core:title: Features that are common to the English law Annex, 2016 CSA VM, English law Deed and New York law Annex

core:listitem.core:para: credit support is provided by one counterparty delivering assets to the other
they all secure the net exposure of one counterparty to the other under a specific master agreement

the arrangements are bilateral—each counterparty could be a provider of collateral at one point in time and a receiver of collateral at another point in time, depending on the net exposure between the counterparties, and
the collateral required under the credit support is periodically adjusted on a mark-to-market basis

tr:secsub1.core:title: 1995 ISDA Credit Support Annex (Transfer—English law), 2013 ISDA Standard Credit Support Annex (Transfer—English law) and 2016 CSA VM
tr:secsub1.core:para: The English law Annex, the English law SCSA and the 2016 CSA VM:

core:listitem.core:para: do not create a security interest—they constitute an outright transfer of title to collateral
form part of the schedule to the master agreement, and
constitute a transaction (as defined in the ISDA Master Agreement)

tr:secsub1.core:para: For more information, see Practice Note: ISDA Credit Support Annex: Transfer—English law.

tr:secsub1.core:title: 1995 ISDA Credit Support Deed (Security Interest—English law)

core:listitem.core:para: creates a security interest in the collateral
does not form part of the schedule to the master agreement—it is a standalone agreement, and
constitutes a credit support document (as defined in the ISDA Master Agreement)

tr:secsub1.core:para: For more information, see Practice Note: ISDA Credit Support Deed: Security Interest—English law.

tr:secsub1.core:title: 1994 ISDA Credit Support Annex (Security Interest—New York law) and 2013 ISDA Standard Credit Support Annex (Security Interest—New York law)
tr:secsub1.core:para: The New York law Annex and New York law SCSA:

core:listitem.core:para: grants a security interest in the collateral
forms part of the schedule to the master agreement, and
constitutes a credit support document (as defined in the ISDA Master Agreement)

tr:secsub1.core:para: For more information, see Practice Note: ISDA Credit Support Annex: Security Interest—New York law.

tr:secmain.core:title: Which credit support documents relate to initial and variation margin?
tr:secmain.core:para: European Market Infrastructure Regulation (EU) 648/2012Assimilated Regulation (EU) 648/2012The Dodd Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act 2010In 2016, ISDA introduced a new suite of credit support documents which are designed to facilitate compliance with margin requirements for derivatives that are not subject to mandatory clearing under the European Market Infrastructure Regulation (EU) 648/2012 (OJ L 201, 27.7.2012, p. 1) (EU EMIR) in the EU, Assimilated Regulation (EU) 648/2012 (UK EMIR) in the UK and the Dodd Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (the Dodd Frank Act) in the US.

The 2016 Phase One credit support documents for IM allow parties subject to regulatory margin requirements on 1 September 2016 (Phase One Entities) to establish IM arrangements that meet the requirements on margin for uncleared swaps. The documents are designed specifically for use between Phase One Entities and act in the same way as the corresponding 'standard' credit support document eg the 2016 Phase One Credit Support Annex governed by New York law also grants a security interest, forms part of the schedule to the master agreement and constitutes a credit support document, just as the 1994 ISDA Credit Support Annex governed by New York law does.

The 2018 credit support documents for IM (comprising the 2018 ISDA Credit Support Annex for Initial Margin (IM) (Security Interest—New York Law) and 2018 Credit Support Deed for Initial Margin (IM) (Security Interest—English Law) are adapted from the 2016 forms for use by buy- and sell-side firms in view of the expansion of the impact of IM regulations to firms coming into scope since September 2019 (Phase 4 onwards).The 2019 Bank Custodian Collateral Transfer Agreement for Initial Margin and forms of Security Agreement are used to document a collateral arrangement between two parties where the collateral is held in a bank custodian account for use in complying with initial margin requirements.

The credit support documents relating to variation margin are updated versions of the corresponding credit support document (eg the 1994 ISDA Credit Support Annex governed by New York law and 1995 ISDA Credit Support Annex governed by English law). However, the documents are limited to variation margin. Through these credit support documents, parties can establish variation margin arrangements that meet the requirements of new regulations on margin for uncleared swaps.

In July 2019, ISDA published template collateral schedules which provide counterparties with pre-selected lists of collateral types and minimum regulatory haircuts. These are designed to be used as a starting point for negotiation between parties in scope for regulatory IM.

On 8 December 2021, ISDA published supplemental definitions for use with ISDA’s regulatory margin documentation, to assist market participants in incorporating descriptions of applicable regulatory regimes into their regulatory IM bilateral agreements, as new regimes have come into force since the publication of those credit support documents.
For more information on the credit support documents for IM and VM, see news analysis: Uncleared derivatives—IM and VM credit support documentation.
The following table summarises the ISDA credit support documents for IM:

entry.core:para: Document Name
Description
2016 ISDA Credit Support Annex for Initial Margin (Security Interest—New York Law)
Designed for Phase One Entities to establish IM arrangements that meet regulatory requirements for uncleared swaps.
2016 ISDA Credit Support Deed for Initial Margin (Security Interest—English Law)
Similar to the New York law version but governed by English law.
2018 ISDA Credit Support Annex for Initial Margin (Security Interest—New York Law)
Adapted from the 2016 forms for use by buy- and sell-side firms coming into scope since September 2019 (Phase 4 onwards).
2018 ISDA Credit Support Deed for Initial Margin (Security Interest—English Law)
Similar to the New York law version but governed by English law.
2019 Bank Custodian Collateral Transfer Agreement for Initial Margin
Used to document collateral arrangements where collateral is held in a bank custodian account.
2019 Forms of Security Agreement
Used in conjunction with the Bank Custodian Collateral Transfer Agreement for Initial Margin.
2019 Template Collateral Schedules
Provide counterparties with pre-selected lists of collateral types and minimum regulatory haircuts, designed as a starting point for negotiation between parties in scope for regulatory IM.
2021 Supplemental Definitions

Assist market participants in incorporating descriptions of applicable regulatory regimes into their regulatory IM bilateral agreements, as new regimes have come into force since the publication of those documents.

tr:secmain.core:para: The following table summarises the ISDA credit support documents for VM:

entry.core:para: Document Name
Description
1994 ISDA Credit Support Annex (New York Law)
Updated for variation margin requirements.
1995 ISDA Credit Support Annex (English Law)
Updated for variation margin requirements.
2016 ISDA Variation Margin Protocol
Helps market participants comply with new rules on margin for uncleared swaps by providing a scalable solution to amend derivatives contract documentation with multiple counterparties.

tr:secmain.core:para: For more information on margin for uncleared swaps generally, see Practice Note: UK EMIR—essentials—Margin requirements and Articles: Saying how versus saying what: the choice of regulatory style in margin policy (2022) 5 JIBFL 335, Challenges ahead of the initial margin “big bang” (2019) 7 JIBFL 453, How soon is now? Release of collateral in initial margin arrangements (2019) 7 JIBFL 431, The future: margining uncleared derivatives (2013) 11 JIBFL 705 and Margin for error (2016) 6 JIBFL 351.

tr:secmain.core:title: ISDA Regulatory Initial Margin AANA Self-Disclosure Letter
tr:secmain.core:para: ISDA Regulatory Initial Margin AANA Self-Disclosure Letter (18 June 2021)In June 2021, ISDA published the ISDA Regulatory Initial Margin AANA Self-Disclosure Letter and accompanying Answer Sheet which is intended to provide a standard form for market participants to communicate certain information needed to determine which trading relationships are, or may become, subject to regulatory IM requirements, and how IM calculations will need to be performed. It allows entities to disclose information identifying whether it is a member of a group and which AANA thresholds (by regime) the entity (or its group) has exceeded for the relevant year. From this information, parties can determine how much IM needs to be paid to comply with IM obligations.

lnci:cite.lnci:content: The initial margin “big bang”: the aftermath (2023) 7 JIBFL 488
Saying how versus saying what: the choice of regulatory style in margin policy (2022) 5 JIBFL 335
Challenges ahead of the initial margin “big bang” (2019) 7 JIBFL 453
How soon is now? Release of collateral in initial margin arrangements (2019) 7 JIBFL 431
Margin for error (2016) 6 JIBFL 351
The future: margining uncleared derivatives (2013) 11 JIBFL 705
Credit Support Legal Risk and Documentation: Henderson on Derivatives [9.18]
Credit Support Legal Risk and Documentation: Henderson on Derivatives [9.18]
The rising risks and roles of financial collateral (2014) 1 JIBFL 3
The future: margining uncleared derivatives (2013) 11 JIBFL 705
Collateral and OTC derivatives after the credit crunch (2009) 5 JIBFL 262
1995 ISDA credit support annex (bilateral form—transfer): The Law and Practice of True Sale, para 25.01
Credit Support Providers and Credit Support Documents: Close-outs: The Law and Market Practice of OTC Derivative Terminations [2.2]–[2.12]
The effect of early termination on ISDA Credit Support Documents: Close-outs: The Law and Market Practice of OTC Derivative Terminations [4.50]–[4.71]
